sections.heading: What is the Collections Framework?
sections.content:
The Java Collections Framework provides data structures and algorithms for storing, retrieving and manipulating groups of objects. It is organized around interfaces (what you can do) and implementations (how it's done): 
Iterable
  └── Collection
        ├── List    → ordered, allows duplicates
        ├── Set     → unique elements
        └── Queue   → FIFO processing

Map (separate hierarchy) → key-value pairs
Golden rule: Declare using the interface, instantiate using the implementation: 
List<String> stops = new ArrayList<>();  // ✓ program to interface
ArrayList<String> stops = new ArrayList<>();  // ✗ coupled to implementation

sections.heading: When to use which?
sections.content:
NeedUseExample
Ordered items, may have duplicatesArrayListDelivery stops in order
Unique items, no order neededHashSetAssigned driver IDs today
Unique items, sortedTreeSetDelivery times sorted
Key → Value lookupHashMapCache delivery by ID
Key → Value, sorted keysTreeMapSchedule by time window
Insertion-order mapLinkedHashMapRecently accessed cache
FIFO processingArrayDequeTask queue

sections.heading: ArrayList
sections.content:
Backed by a resizable array. The default choice for most use cases. Performance: • get(index): O(1) — direct array access • add(element): O(1) amortized (resizes when full, doubles capacity) • add(index, element): O(n) — shifts elements right • remove(index): O(n) — shifts elements left • contains(element): O(n) — linear scan 
List<String> stops = new ArrayList<>();
stops.add("Warehouse");       // append at end
stops.add("Store A");
stops.add(1, "Priority");     // insert at index 1, shifts others
stops.get(0);                 // "Warehouse" — O(1)
stops.remove(0);              // removes "Warehouse", shifts left
stops.size();                 // 2
When to use: Almost always. CPU cache locality makes it faster than LinkedList even for insertions in practice.

sections.heading: LinkedList
sections.content:
Doubly-linked list. Each element points to previous and next. Performance: • get(index): O(n) — must traverse from start/end • add at start/end: O(1) • add/remove in middle (if you have the node): O(1) • contains: O(n) 
LinkedList<String> queue = new LinkedList<>();
queue.addFirst("urgent");     // O(1) at head
queue.addLast("normal");      // O(1) at tail
queue.removeFirst();          // O(1) poll from head
When to use: Rarely. Only when you need frequent add/remove at both ends (use as Deque). For most list operations, ArrayList wins due to cache performance.

sections.heading: Key interview point
sections.content: "When would you use LinkedList over ArrayList?" The honest answer: Almost never in practice. ArrayList has better cache locality (elements contiguous in memory). LinkedList has pointer overhead per element. Even for frequent insertions, ArrayList usually wins. Exception: Using it as a Deque (double-ended queue) where you only add/remove from ends. But ArrayDeque is often better there too. The real interview insight: Know the Big-O differences, but also know that in practice, ArrayList dominates because modern CPUs heavily favor sequential memory access.

sections.heading: HashMap — The Workhorse
sections.content:
Stores key-value pairs. O(1) average for get/put. How it works internally: 1. Call key.hashCode() → integer 2. Compute bucket index: hash & (capacity - 1) 3. If bucket empty → store entry directly 4. If bucket occupied → collision: • Java 7: linked list in bucket • Java 8+: linked list → red-black tree when chain > 8 entries 
Map<Long, Delivery> cache = new HashMap<>();
cache.put(1001L, delivery);              // store
Delivery d = cache.get(1001L);           // O(1) lookup
cache.containsKey(1001L);                // O(1)
cache.getOrDefault(9999L, fallback);     // default if missing
cache.putIfAbsent(1001L, newDelivery);   // only if key missing

// Iteration:
for (Map.Entry<Long, Delivery> e : cache.entrySet()) {
    Long id = e.getKey();
    Delivery del = e.getValue();
}
Critical: equals/hashCode contract If you use a custom object as key: • If a.equals(b) → a.hashCode() == b.hashCode() MUST be true • Without this, HashMap cannot find your entries

sections.heading: TreeMap — Sorted Keys
LinkedHashMap — Insertion Order
HashSet — Unique Elements
sections.content:
Backed by HashMap internally (values are a dummy constant). O(1) add/contains/remove. 
Set<String> assignedDrivers = new HashSet<>();
assignedDrivers.add("driver-101");   // true (added)
assignedDrivers.add("driver-101");   // false (duplicate ignored)
assignedDrivers.contains("driver-101");  // O(1)
assignedDrivers.size();              // 1
When to use: When the business rule IS uniqueness — unique stops, unique IDs, deduplication. Requires: Proper equals() and hashCode() on the element type. Without them, "equal" objects can appear as duplicates.

sections.heading: Useful operations
sections.content:
Set<String> a = new HashSet<>(List.of("A", "B", "C"));
Set<String> b = new HashSet<>(List.of("B", "C", "D"));

// Union
Set<String> union = new HashSet<>(a);
union.addAll(b);  // [A, B, C, D]

// Intersection
Set<String> common = new HashSet<>(a);
common.retainAll(b);  // [B, C]

// Difference
Set<String> onlyA = new HashSet<>(a);
onlyA.removeAll(b);  // [A]
Interview tip: "Find common elements between two lists" → convert to Sets and use retainAll(). O(n) instead of O(n²) nested loops.

sections.heading: What are Streams?
Common operations
sections.content:
Intermediate (return a stream — lazy): 
.filter(predicate)        // keep elements matching condition
.map(function)            // transform each element
.flatMap(function)        // flatten nested collections
.sorted()                 // natural order
.sorted(comparator)       // custom order
.distinct()               // remove duplicates
.limit(n)                 // take first n
.skip(n)                  // skip first n
.peek(consumer)           // debug: look without consuming
Terminal (trigger processing — produce result): 
.collect(Collectors.toList())    // to List
.collect(Collectors.toSet())     // to Set
.collect(Collectors.groupingBy(fn))  // to Map<K, List<V>>
.count()                         // number of elements
.findFirst()                     // Optional<T>
.anyMatch(predicate)             // boolean: any match?
.allMatch(predicate)             // boolean: all match?
.forEach(consumer)               // side effect (use carefully)
.reduce(identity, accumulator)   // combine all into one value
.toList()                        // Java 16+ shortcut

sections.heading: Real examples
sections.content:
// Group deliveries by status
Map<Status, List<Delivery>> byStatus = deliveries.stream()
    .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(Delivery::getStatus));

// Total weight of all deliveries
double totalWeight = deliveries.stream()
    .mapToDouble(Delivery::getWeight)
    .sum();

// Find the delivery with longest distance
Optional<Delivery> longest = deliveries.stream()
    .max(Comparator.comparing(Delivery::getDistance));

// Unique driver names, sorted
List<String> drivers = deliveries.stream()
    .map(Delivery::getDriverName)
    .distinct()
    .sorted()
    .collect(Collectors.toList());

// Comma-separated IDs
String ids = deliveries.stream()
    .map(d -> d.getId().toString())
    .collect(Collectors.joining(", "));

sections.heading: Optional — No more NullPointerException
Core rules
sections.content: A subclass inherits accessible state and behavior from its parent. Constructors are not inherited; the parent constructor is invoked with super. Java supports single class inheritance and multiple interface implementation.

sections.heading: Overriding
sections.content: An overriding method keeps a compatible signature and cannot reduce visibility. Covariant return types are allowed. Use @Override to let the compiler detect mistakes.

sections.heading: Polymorphism
sections.content: The declared reference type controls what members are accessible at compile time; the runtime object type selects the overridden method implementation.

sections.heading: Guidance
sections.content: Prefer composition unless the relationship is a stable and honest is-a relationship that satisfies the Liskov Substitution Principle.
